coredata.dc:title: Lac-extract doped polyaniline nano-ribbons as fluorescence sensor and molecular switch for detection of aqueous AsO4 3− and Fe3+ contaminants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Adhikari, Arpita
Mukherjee, Somali
Chakraborty, Amit K.
Biswas, Suman
Basu, Arijita
Chakraborty, Mukut
Chattopadhyay, Sanatan
Das, Debasis
Chattopadhyay, Dipankar
coredata.dc:description: Detection of contaminants dissolved in water is an essential step for water quality monitoring and can guide us from consuming unhealthy water. Herein, we demonstrate the development of a novel low-cost polymer by doping polyaniline nanoribbons with lac-extract (LAC-PANI) and report its excellent fluorescence sensing properties against aqueous AsO4 3− and Fe3+ ions, both of which AsO4 3− and Fe3+can pose health hazard for humans with the former being extremely toxic. When excited with radiation of 320 nm, the sensor showed contrasting fluorescence response against AsO4 3− and Fe3+ ions as the luminescence intensity showed sharp quenching upon exposure to Fe3+ but against AsO4 3− it showed a sharp rise in intensity. When other cations and oxoanions were concurrently tested with Fe3+ and AsO4 3− respectively, the sensor showed high specificity only to these two ions. Furthermore, a reversible and distinct fluorescence turn on–off-on switching response was observed upon sequential addition of AsO4 3−, Fe3+ and AsO4 3− ions which points to the utility of the sensor as a NOT-AND compound molecular logic gate for simultaneous detection of two electronically divergent analyte classes. Thus, the LAC-PANI sensor developed in this work shows enormous potential for next generation fluorescence sensor useful for water quality monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensing
Molecular switch
Polyaniline
Lac-extract
Water quality monitoring

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of laccaic acid doped polyaniline (LAC-PANI)
Chemical analysis by FTIR and XPS
Specificity and reusability of the sensor
Designing of the molecular switch (Logic gate)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of PANI (a) and LAC-PANI (b).
(a) FTIR spectra of LAC-extract, Oxidized LAC, PANI and LAC-PANI, (b) N1s XPS spectrum of LAC-PANI deconvoluted into various components.
Fluorescence spectra of different intrinsic samples.

Comparison of Luminescence intensities of LAC-PANI in presence of a) Fe3+and other cations, b) AsO4 3− and other oxoanions. c) Fluorescence quenching as a function of Fe3+ concentration [inset the Stern-Volmer plot], d) Fluorescence enhancement as a function of AsO4 3− concentration [inset determination of enhancement constant]. λex = 320 nm. The spectra were recorded in aqueous medium containing 5 mg of LAC-PANI dispersed in 25 mL of water.
Time resolved fluorescence decay of LAC-PANI before and after addition of; a) Fe3+,b) AsO4 3−.
Efficiencies (in %) of Fluorescence quenching against cations (for Fe3+) and enhancement against oxoanions (for AsO4 3−) of the LAC-PANI sensor.
Switching fluorescence upon sequential addition of AsO4 3−, Fe3+ and again AsO4 3−. All spectra were recorded on samples prepared by dispersing 5 mg of LAC-PANI in 25 mL water.

(a) Circuit for the coupled NOT-AND logic gate representing the fluorescence responses of the LAC-PANI molecular switch against Fe3+ and AsO4 3−, (b) Schematics of the fluorescence responses of LAC-PANI for the sequential addition of Fe3+ and AsO4 3−.
Truth table for the LAC-PANI NAND logic gate molecular switch.

head.ce:title: Lac-extract doped polyaniline nano-ribbons as fluorescence sensor and molecular switch for detection of aqueous AsO4 3− and Fe3+ contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Science, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Kolkata 700009, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic Science, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Kolkata 700009, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of contaminants dissolved in water is an essential step for water quality monitoring and can guide us from consuming unhealthy water. Herein, we demonstrate the development of a novel low-cost polymer by doping polyaniline nanoribbons with lac-extract (LAC-PANI) and report its excellent fluorescence sensing properties against aqueous AsO4 3− and Fe3+ ions, both of which AsO4 3− and Fe3+can pose health hazard for humans with the former being extremely toxic. When excited with radiation of 320 nm, the sensor showed contrasting fluorescence response against AsO4 3− and Fe3+ ions as the luminescence intensity showed sharp quenching upon exposure to Fe3+ but against AsO4 3− it showed a sharp rise in intensity. When other cations and oxoanions were concurrently tested with Fe3+ and AsO4 3− respectively, the sensor showed high specificity only to these two ions. Furthermore, a reversible and distinct fluorescence turn on–off-on switching response was observed upon sequential addition of AsO4 3−, Fe3+ and AsO4 3− ions which points to the utility of the sensor as a NOT-AND compound molecular logic gate for simultaneous detection of two electronically divergent analyte classes. Thus, the LAC-PANI sensor developed in this work shows enormous potential for next generation fluorescence sensor useful for water quality monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Clean water is one of the essential tenets to lead healthy life. Nevertheless, the unrestrained use, rapid industrialization and the cynical behaviour of humans have caused severe water pollution leading to a swift decline of consumable water [1,2]. The ever growing industrial wastes often containing toxic chemicals when disposed directly to water bodies without pre-treatment leads to incessant water related environmental pollution [3–5]. Sensitive as well as selective detection of waterborne chemical hazards is very important for water quality monitoring [6,7]. Among various water pollutants, Arsenic is one of the most toxic and deadliest contaminants which pose severe threat to human health and other living organisms [8–14]. It can render the normal life at risk due to its detrimental impacts like birth defects, cardio vascular complications, nephropathy, skin lesions, carcinogenic effects etc [15–23]. Arsenic contamination in ground water is a well-known issue requiring attention [24] as the search for a portable and cost effective method for its detection and removal is still on.

Far fetching from arsenic, iron (Fe3+), a ubiquitous metal ion is constantly liberated into water from the growing industries and different anthropogenic activities leading to an increase in its concentration in some water sources. Although iron is an essential element for life metabolism (as it plays an indispensible role in oxygen transport and storage, electron transfer [25–29], its concentration in potable water should be around ∼5.357 µmol/L [30,31]. The overload or inadequacy of iron can endanger disorder and diseases like anaemia, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s and so on [32–34].

Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple low-cost, selective [35]and convenient sensors for prompt detection of these contaminants for water quality monitoring. Fluorometric sensing is a technique that is gaining increasing attention due to its fast response, high repeatability and accuracy [36–41]. As a sensing material, conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), Polypyrrole (PPY) have generated large research interest in recent years due to its ease of synthesis, high conductivity, low cost and wide applicability in the realm of sensors [42–44] supercapacitors, etc. [45–47]. Conducting polymers can also work as fluorescence sensor when suitably doped or functionalised with luminescent groups [48].

In view of the above, here we demonstrate a simple method to synthesize PANI nanoribbons doped with a naturally occurring acid, laccaic acid extracted from Lac dye with an aim to explore its performance as a fluorescence sensor for two common water polluting ions, viz, AsO4 3−, Fe3+. We could not find any previous literature reporting the use of the naturally obtained Lac dye in combination with PANI for sensing purposes. The synthesized material not only showed strong fluorescence sensing against the above two analytes but exhibited contrasting turn on-turn off fluorescence responses against the two oppositely charged ions when concomitantly present. A radical turn on–off-on fluorescence switching was observed upon consecutive addition of AsO4 3−/ Fe3+/ AsO4 3− to the LAC-PANI solution. This reversible switching behavior was further demonstrated as a compound NOT-AND logic switch that can simultaneously detect both these ions. To the best of our knowledge, fabrication of such fluorescence switching device made of a conducting polymer for simultaneous detection of two electronically diverse analytes is unreported.

Aniline, Ammonium per sulfate and Hydrochloric acid were purchased from Merck India Ltd. Sodium Arsenate and ferric chloride were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Solid LAC was collected from shellac (secreted by lac bug, Kerrialacca). Aniline was purified by distillation and kept at 4 °C in Ar atmosphere prior to use and triple distilled water was used in the entire work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of laccaic acid doped polyaniline (LAC-PANI)
ce:section.ce:para: LAC is a scarlet dye mainly present in live insects (Laccifer lacca; syn. Kerria lacca) produced in a resinous cocoon known as sticklac as a secretion from the lac insect. According to Pattanayak, LAC dye contains a mixture of laccaic acids, viz., laccaic acid A (71–96%), laccaic acid B (0–20%) with minor components of laccaic acids C, D and E [49]. In this work, a stock solution of LAC dye was prepared by soaking 2.5 g of solid LAC (shellac) in 20 mL of water. After overnight soaking the soluble dye was filtered through Whatmann filter paper (no. 42) and a bright red colored extract was collected and stored at 4 °C (hereafter called laccaic acid).

To prepare LAC-PANI, an appropriate amount(13 mL) of the aforesaid filtered LAC extract was taken in a beaker containing 20 mL of water at temperature ∼0° – 4 °C to which 0.2 mL of aniline monomer was added followed by addition of 0.5 g APS (dissolved in 7 mL water) to initiate polymerization. Here, laccaic acid performed multiple roles: as a reducing agent (to be oxidized by APS), as a soft template for governing the specific nano morphology of PANI, and as a dopant for PANI. Upon addition of APS, the LAC extract solution changed its color from wine red to transparent orange and eventually turned dark green accompanied by a phase separation after 4 h as the polymerization completed suggesting formation of laccaic acid doped polyaniline (LAC-PANI). The probable structure of LAC-PANI is shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting information. The resultant dark green LAC-PANI was isolated by filtration followed by repeated washing with water and acetone to remove traces of unconsumed monomer, APS, oligoaniline, residual laccaic acid, etc. The isolated LAC-PANI was then dried in a vacuum desiccators for 24 h before further analysis. The control sample (normal PANI) was prepared by adding 0.2 mL aniline monomer to 33 mL HCl 1 (M) solution to which 7 mL aqueous APS (0.5 gm) was added to initiate polymerization and continued for 4 h. At The reaction product was collected through filtration and subsequent drying in a vacuum dessicator before for further studies.

SEM experiment was performed in a scanning electron microscope (ZEISS EVO 18) after the samples were sputter coated with a thin platinum film to arrest charging. FT-IR measurement was conducted on pallets of LAC-PANI dispersed in KBr using a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 2). XPS was performed in a Thermo-VG Scientific ESCA Lab 250 microprobe spectrometer using monochromated Al Kα source radiating x-rays of 1486.6 eV.

The fluorescence spectral studies were carried out using 320 nm excitation energy in a fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA Fluorolog-3). Samples for fluorescence sensing were prepared by sonicating 5 mg of LAC-PANI in 25 mL of double distilled water for 40 min. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm. For sensing of the Fe3+ ions, 1 mM aqueous solutions of the chloride salts of Fe3+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Li+ and Cr3+ were used. To test the sensor performance against AsO4 3− ions, various toxic oxoanion were checked using 1 mM aqueous solutions of sodium or potassium salts of PO4 3−, AsO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3 –, NO2 –, MoO4 2−, MnO4 −, ClO4 −, CrO4 2−, and Cr2O7 2−. For specificity study Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+etc. had been added along with Fe3+ and for anion PO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3 –, NO2 –, MoO4 2−, MnO4 −, ClO4 −, CrO4 2−, and Cr2O7 2− etc. had been added along with AsO4 3−. For fluorescence switching experiment, initially 100 µL of AsO4 3− (1 mM) solution was added to LAC-PANI followed by 100 µL of Fe3+(1 mM) solution. To check the reversibility again 100 µL AsO4 3− (1 mM) solution was added to the resultant mixture.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the SEM micrograph of the PANI reference sample whereas Fiure 1(b) shows the ribbonlike morphology of LAC-PANI with typical ribbon widths of ∼100 nm. A possible pathway for formation of LAC-PANI is described in Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information in which it is speculated that the oxidized LAC and aniline monomer interacts through hydrogen bonding and hydrophilic interactions involving –COOH and other polar groups of the laccaic acid along with simultaneous hydrophobic expulsion [50–52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis by FTIR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (a) compares the FTIR spectra of PANI, LAC-extract (laccaic acid), oxidized LAC and LAC-PANI in which a broad band is observed within the range 3600–2900 cm−1 due to overlap of IR frequencies of various bonds. The oxidised LAC shows some shift in IR frequency (3500–2850 cm−1) indicating lowering of O–H stretching due to greater opportunity of hydrogen bonding interaction with the newly introduced –OH groups upon oxidation. In case of LAC-PANI, new bands appear around 3233 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 (which are absent in PANI) can be attributed to the presence of such phenolic ‘–OH’ group of oxidised LAC indicating the success of doping of PANI. LAC-PANI also shows peaks around 1561 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1due to C = C stretching of quinoid and benzenoid rings respectively. The peaks centered at 1292 cm−1 and 1113 cm−1 are associated with the C-N stretching of aromatic amine and N-Q-N stretching frequences [53,54].

Since XPS is a powerful technique for identification of chemical states of elements present in a material, we performed XPS of LAC-PANI. As both Laccaic acid and PANI contain C, H, O and N it is difficult to identify their origins in the resultant LAC-PANI material. Since nitrogen is found in different chemical environment in laccaic acid and PANI (Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information), we tried to fit the N1s XPS spectrum into its components and used it to evaluate the success of doping of PANI. Fig. 2b plots the N1s XPS spectrum fitted using a Gaussian-Lorentzian mixed function (70:30) showing the curves representing nitrogen signal originating from different chemical states of nitrogen. A Shirley type background was added to account for the changing background signal of the spectrum in accordance with established literature [55]. The binding energies were calibrated by aligning the position of the main peak of C1s spectrum with 284.7 eV [56]. The N1s spectrum shows three main components: the component peaking at 400.0 eV originates from the amine (–NH–) groups of PANI and laccaic acid, whereas the components at 401.7 eV and 403.2 eV originate from the two protonated imine groups (positively charged = N+). This is in accordance with previous work reporting XPS of doped PANI with organic acids like p-tolune sulfonic acid and dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid [57]. This confirms the successful conversion of the imine groups of PANI by laccaic acid and in turn confirms the formation of LAC doped PANI (LAC-PANI). It is worth noting that due to presence of amine groups in both PANI and laccaic acid, an accurate estimation of doping level would be difficult and hence we did not attempt it.

The fluorescence spectrum of oxidized LAC showed (Fig. 3 ) considerable emission around 420 nm when excited with radiation of 320 nm. Under identical experimental condition many fold increase in the emission intensity was observed from LAC-PANI with a blue shift of λmax (Fig. 3). The quantum yield of LAC-PANI was estimated to be 0.016 (Details of calculation and values can be found in Supporting Information, Table 1). This enhanced emission intensity associated with LAC-PANI could be attributed to two simultaneous effects: i) upon doping extended conjugation leads to enhance the delocalization of π electrons in the hybrid matrix leading to enhance the emission intensity [58,59] and ii) with the formation of LAC-PANI the probability of solution phase aggregate formation of oxidized laccaic acid decreases, increasing the isolation of acid core among LAC-PANI chain which may diminish the loss of excitation energy and may in turn enhance the luminescence intensity [58,60].

The ability of aqueous phase detection is one of the foremost criteria to develop real time sensing probe. Encouraged by the good stability and strong luminescence of LAC-PANI in water, the fluorescence sensing experiments were conducted by dissolving the analytes in water. At the onset, the produced LAC-PANI material was treated with 1 mM solutions of different metal ions in its chloride form. Interestingly, fluorescence intensity was observed to quench immediately for the solution containing Fe3+ions whereas for solutions containing other ions only negligible change in luminescence intensity (Fig. 4 a) was noticed. This data clearly points to the highly selective nature of LAC-PANI towards Fe3+ over the entire range of cations investigated by us. The total quenching phenomena was monitored by an incremental addition of Fe 3+ solution in the dispersion of LAC-PANI and the total turn off fluorescence quenching was observed only upon addition of 250 µL (1.25 mM) of the Fe3+ solution (Fig. 4c). The photographs of LAC, LAC-PANI, LAC-PANI + Fe3+, and LAC-PANI + AsO4 3− under natural light and UV light (320 nm) are shown in Fig. S3 of the Supporting Information.

For careful evaluation of the titration profile the data were further fitted to the Stern-Volmer plot given by,
(1)
I 0
/ I =
K
sv
Q
+ 1
where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after addition of the analyte, [Q] is the concentration of the added analyte, and KSV is a quenching constant.

As depicted in the inset diagram of Fig. 4c, a linear increment of the curve with the correlation coefficient 0.96 is observed specifying the KSV value 3.69 × 104 M−1. Such high quenching constant could be an amalgamated effect of various unprecedented interaction of Fe3+ with the pendent –OH and –NHCO functionalities of negatively charged dopant on the surface of LAC-PANI emeraldine salt. To investigate the interaction of LAC-PANI with oxoanions, the fluorescence spectra of the LAC-PANI was constantly monitored as a function of addition of various oxoanionic solutions (Fig. 4b). In contrast with the previous observation, a sharp enhancement of fluorescence intensity of LAC-PANI was observed upon incremental addition of AsO4 3− solution. Remarkably the emission intensity increases ∼ 3 times just upon the addition of 100 µL (0.05 mM) of the AsO4 3− solution (Fig. 4d).

We tried to also evaluate the intensity enhancement phenomena quantitatively and the emission enhancement constant (KEC) was calculated using the equation,
(2)
I /
I 0
=
K
EC
Q
+ 1
where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity before and after addition of the analyte, [Q] is the concentration of the added analyte. From the linear fitting of the curve the KEC value was calculated to be 4.14 × 104 M−1 which surely indicates efficient electrostatic interaction of AsO4 3− with the cationic PANI surface of LAC-PANI (inset in Fig. 4d). In fact, such elevated optical signal with high KEC makes the synthesized material appropriate for real time detection of AsO4 3− too. The Stokes Shift of LAC-PANI was calculated to be 7611.20 cm−1 which changed to 8081.25 cm−1 and 7668.66 cm−1in presence of Fe3+ and AsO4 3−.

Since limit of detection (LOD) is an important qualifier for a good sensor, we estimated the LOD of the LAC-PANI sensor as follows:
(3)
LOD =
3 σ / m
where the standard deviation (σ) was calculated by measuring five successive fluorescence intensities of LAC-PANI in absence of any added analyte (under identical condition). The slopes (m) were obtained by plotting the emission intensities of LAC-PANI against concentrations of added analytes (AsO4 3−& Fe3+) from the plots in Figure S2 of the Supporting Information. The LOD values as estimated from Eq. (3) were 0.64 nM for Fe3+and 0.37 nM for AsO4 3− (Table S2, supporting information). To the best of our knowledge these values are comparable or lower than the previously reported PANI based Fe3+ and AsO4 3−sensors. More interestingly, the obtained LOD values are distinctly lower than the threshold of iron and arsenate in drinking water as recommended by World Health Organization (WHO) manifesting the utility of LAC-PANI sensor [61].

To elucidate the detection mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence measurements were comprehensively carried out with aqueous dispersion of LAC-PANI before and after addition of different concentration of the analytes like Fe3+ and AsO4 3− (Fig. 5 and Table S3 in Supporting information file). The average decay lifetime of LAC-PANI was observed to be 3.66 ns. Upon addition of 30 µL 1 mM Fe3+ solution the average decay time of LAC-PANI changed to 2.48 ns. With increase in the concentration of added quencher (60 µL) the average life time value decreased further to 1.69 ns. In case of AsO4 3−the average decay lifetime of LAC-PANI were observed to be 2.09 ns and 1.94 ns upon addition of 30 µL and 60 µL of 1 mM AsO4 3− solution, respectively. The steady decrease of average decay lifetime of LAC-PANI with increasing concentration of the analytes indicates that the sensing phenomena are dynamic in nature in both the cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reusability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As the specificity and reusability are fundamental to practical use of any sensor, we explored the LAC-PANI sensor in the concomitant presence of other cations for Fe3+ sensing and oxoanions for AsO4 3− sensing, the results of which are shown in Fig. 6 . It is interesting to observe only negligible change in fluorescence emission intensity for all ions other than Fe3+ and AsO4 3− even when other analytes were simultaneously present which demonstrates a high specificity of LAC-PANI against Fe3+ and AsO4 3−.

To test the long term stability and the reusability of the sensor, the LAC-PANI material was recovered through filtration after each sensing cycle, washed gently with distilled water and dried. The recovered powdered material was again tested for sensing only to observe negligible change in its sensing performance against Fe3+ and AsO4 3−. The FTIR analysis conducted on the samples obtained after third recovery cycle also revealed that the spectrum remained unchanged as that of the pristine material in both the cases of Fe3+ and AsO4 3− (Fig. S4 of the supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing of the molecular switch (Logic gate)
ce:section.ce:para: The contrasting fluorescence response of LAC-PANI towards Fe3+ and AsO4 3− (quenching and enhancement) motivated us to explore its performance when both Fe3+ and AsO4 3− ions are concomitantly present. For this we monitored the change of emission intensity of LAC-PANI pre-treated with AsO4 3− solution upon addition of Fe3+ solution. It was remarkable to note an instantaneous switching of the luminescence intensity upon addition of Fe3+ ions into the pre-treated solution. What was even more interesting was that upon further addition of AsO4 3− to the resultant quenched solution, the emission intensity rapidly increased again (Fig. 7 ) indicating a clear turn on–off–on or off–on–off (for reverse procedure) recognition of the aforesaid contaminants in water circumventing complications like separation, regeneration etc.

Considering the reversible turn on–off-on switching action of the LAC-PANI sensor against its two inputs ‘A’ (AsO4 3−) and ‘B’ (Fe3+), a molecular logic switch was designed (Fig. 8 a) such that the output Y is the change in the resultant fluorescence intensity (quenching or enhancement). The logic device that can represent the fluorescence switching of LAC-PANI against AsO4 3− and Fe3+ ions is that of a compound NOT-AND gate whose output is expressed by the equation,
(4)
Y = A
B
-
.

The LAC-PANI switch has its characteristics fluorescence (State I = initial state) which decreases with the addition of Fe3+ solution (State II) and increases with AsO4 3− addition (State III) (Fig. 8b). Considering that the addition of Fe3+in AsO4 3−containing solution reduces the fluorescence emission intensity, the overall switching operation can be described by the following truth table (Table 1 ) in which the logic state 1 symbolizes when arsenate works as emission intensity enhancer whereas logic state 0 signifies when Fe3+ works as the emission intensity suppressor. A possible explanation of such unique behavior could be the electrostatic affinity of Fe3+ towards AsO4 3− to form FeAsO4.

In summary, we demonstrated a novel method to develop a fluorescence sensor based on PANI doped with a natural dye collected from Lac-extract. The produced LAC-PANI nano-ribbons displayed sharp fluorescence response (although opposite in character) against two common water pollutants, viz, Fe3+ and AsO4 3− along with low LOD and high specificity. The LAC-PANI sensor also showed good reusability and ultrafast fluorescence switching action making it an excellent fluorescence marker against these two water contaminants. Further, the sensor showed alternating response upon sequential addition of the above two oppositely charged ions which was further utilized to fabricate a compound NOT-AND logic device capable of simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and AsO4 3−which is very interesting. The sensor being easily synthesized from low-cost materials and due to its cumulative features as described above, shows enormous potential for its practical use in water quality monitoring.

Arpita Adhikari: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Validation, Conceptualization, Data curation. Somali Mukherjee: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Amit K. Chakraborty: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Formal analysis. Suman Biswas: Investigation, Formal analysis. Arijita Basu: Data curation. Mukut Chakraborty: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sanatan Chattopadhyay: Visualization, Formal analysis. Debasis Das: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Dipankar Chattopadhyay: Resources, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.A. duly acknowledges West Bengal DST for providing research fellowship. AKC acknowledges the Ministry of Education, Govt. of India for its grant under TEQIP-III (May 2018) to the Centre of Excellence in Advanced Materials at NIT Durgapur for some of the characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114000.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced porous materials for sensing, capture and detoxification of organic pollutants toward water remediation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-throughput multiplexed competitive immunoassay for pollutants sensing in water

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective “turn-on” fluorescence determination of mercury ion in food and environmental samples through novel anthracene and pyrene appended Schiff bases
